name: differentiated instruction gifted struggling students same class
top_issues: C10 — Wayground's value proposition is vague throughout. 'Wayground offers standards-aligned resources with built-in accommodations' and 'Use Wayground's AI features to generate versions at different reading levels' never name a specific feature (e.g., 'Wayground's Create with AI' or a specific tool), leaving teachers with no actionable reason to click or try it. The product might as well be unnamed.

C3 — Several passages read as AI-generated rather than teacher-voiced: 'predictable adaptability,' 'anchoring instruction to one well-defined goal,' and the Wayground CTA blocks feel copy-pasted from a marketing deck. The '200+ Wayground educators' stat and the '78% of teachers who used tiered tasks' figure appear without sourcing context, which a skeptical teacher will distrust immediately.

C5 — Grade-band anchoring is thin. Only one concrete example ('4th-grade teacher at Maple Ridge Elementary') appears in the body. The 90-minute block, tiered task table (particle motion), and choice board examples are never labeled by grade or subject beyond that single anecdote, leaving a 9th-grade ELA or 2nd-grade math teacher unsure whether any of this applies to them.
positive_signals: The tiered/parallel task comparison table is genuinely excellent — a teacher could photocopy it for a PLC meeting. The four-column structure with shared objectives, modality variations, and unified success criteria is exactly how practitioners think about differentiation design.

The 90-minute block blueprint is specific enough to be actionable (7-min launch, 19-min rotations, 7-min synthesis), and the FAQ section addresses real implementation friction — large class sizes, limited materials — that teachers actually face.
narrative: This article has real structural bones — the tiered/parallel task table and the 90-minute rotation blueprint are the kind of concrete tools teachers will actually keep — but it falls short of a PASS on two fronts that matter most to classroom trust. The Wayground mentions are too vague to be useful ("AI features," "built-in accommodations" with no named feature), which reads as marketing filler rather than a genuine tool recommendation, and several passages have an AI-generated cadence ("predictable adaptability," unsourced percentage claims) that a skeptical veteran teacher will clock immediately. To pass, the article needs at least one named Wayground feature with a specific use case tied to differentiation, and the grade-band examples need to extend beyond the single 4th-grade anecdote — ideally adding one secondary and one primary example to give the strategies broader classroom credibility.
